published_prior_art.cite: MartinussenKnudsenRytgaard2026Debiased — exact-time illness-death intervention target, but with a different conditional-hazard functional
KennedyBalakrishnanRobinsWasserman2024MinimaxCATE — Hölder point-target version selection is standard substrate
MartinussenKnudsenRytgaard2026Debiased — its fixed-window intervention is not the arbitrary symmetric-K intervention defined here

repo_prior_art.cite: stat_dose_response_minimax — fixed-dose Hölder local-bump minimax converse

published_prior_art.cite: ChernozhukovChetverikovKato2014Gaussian supplies sufficient coupling conditions, not a necessary-and-sufficient survival-score frontier

novelty_flags.label: def:eif and prop:fixed-window-reduction
novelty_flags.one_line: The core says its arbitrary symmetric-K hazard rule reduces to the Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard fixed-window target/EIF, but that paper uses a window density obtained by truncating and renormalizing the observed initiation distribution, not an arbitrary K density.

soundness_flags.label: def:g-formula, prop:g-formula, and prop:intervention-equivalence
soundness_flags.one_line: A deterministic rule with no initiation before u must set the 01 hazard to zero before u, yet Psi_u retains alpha_01 in its pre-u survival factor; consequently it cannot equal the stated causal risk, and it cannot equal theta_b's intervention risk.

soundness_flags.label: oeq:rate-lower and def:frontier-handle
soundness_flags.one_line: With the declared hazards alpha_02(t|X) and alpha_12(t|X), a bump of amplitude h^s in alpha_02 around u_star changes the displayed integral target by O(h^(s+1)), not h^s; the claimed Le Cam separation and n h^(2s+1) calibration therefore do not follow.

soundness_flags.label: def:g-formula, def:eif, def:one-step, and def:variance-and-process
soundness_flags.one_line: The model omits the initiation-time argument alpha_12(t|u,X) and conditional post-initiation censoring/survival objects that make exact-time treatment nonregular; theta_{b_n(c)}(hat eta) and b(c) are also undefined as written.

soundness_flags.label: ass:sequential-exchangeability
soundness_flags.one_line: The cited Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard paper explicitly treats observed-hazard causal validity as an assumption and assigns its no-unmeasured-common-cause characterization to an unpublished manuscript, so this is not a cited standard condition in the stated form.

soundness_flags.label: ass:localized-rates and def:upper-class
soundness_flags.one_line: Membership of the law class is stated through errors of named fitted procedures (widehat alpha and widehat S), rather than through primitive law-level estimability properties.

accepted_scope_caveats.label: honest_scope: interior known-smoothness regime
accepted_scope_caveats.caveat: Interior u_star, one initiation, known s and L, local positivity, CAR, and bounded hazards are accepted as scope restrictions only.
accepted_scope_caveats.bound_claim: Any rebuilt rate or inference claim is conditional on this fixed interior Hölder illness-death regime; adaptation and general multistate claims remain outside the headline.

source_verification_receipts.locator: pp. 3--6, especially p. 3 lines 114--117, p. 4 equation (2), pp. 4--5 definition of the smoothed intervention, and Theorems 3.1 and 4.1
source_verification_receipts.verified_claim: The source states that alpha_12(t|u) may depend on initiation time, sets the 01 hazard to zero before an exact initiation time, defines its smoothed rule by truncating and renormalizing g(u)=alpha_01(u) exp{-A_01(u)}, and says causal-validity characterization is in a manuscript in preparation.
The paper develops a doubly robust mapping and local-linear kernel smoothing for a continuous-treatment effect curve, with consistency and asymptotic-normality conditions.
The paper gives kernel-localized, cross-fitted doubly robust inference for continuous-treatment dose-response and partial-effect functionals under high-level and low-level nuisance conditions.
The paper studies global and local pointwise dose-response estimation, including a local doubly robust learner and smoothness-sensitive risk bounds.
The paper proves a Hölder CATE minimax lower bound and gives a rate-optimal local-polynomial causal estimator under high-level conditions.
The paper derives EIFs, minimax lower bounds, and concentration-dependent rates for continuous-exposure incremental interventions, with effective sample size n/delta.
The paper establishes pointwise and uniform-in-time asymptotic linearity for a cross-fitted doubly robust estimator of baseline-treatment survival curves.
Theorem 2.1 supplies Gaussian approximation for empirical-process suprema under explicit envelope moments and complexity conditions.
The paper constructs worst-case-bias-aware honest intervals for scalar nonparametric targets and studies bandwidth and length efficiency.
The paper gives a uniform Bahadur representation and bootstrap confidence bands for a weighted local-linear continuous-treatment estimator.

strengthening_paths.suggestion: Rebuild the observed-law target with alpha_12(t|u,X) and any required conditional censoring survival, derive the deterministic-intervention g-formula with alpha_01 suppressed before u, and then re-establish the Hölder-version target.

Either adopt the Martinussen--Knudsen--Rytgaard truncated-natural-initiation window exactly or prove a new EIF for the arbitrary K intervention; remove the claimed fixed-window reduction unless the two interventions are made identical.

Construct the lower-bound perturbation in the initiation-time-indexed post-treatment hazard, verify Hölder membership, target separation h^s, and KL order n h^(2s+1) before retaining the minimax headline.
Replace estimator-dependent membership conditions by law-level localized nuisance-estimability conditions, then introduce the selected learners as inputs to the upper-bound conjecture.

recommended_next_step: Reject this formulation and rebuild the causal functional and lower-bound submodel before re-entering Stage -0.5; the present model removes the initiation-time conditional hazard that carries the intended nonregularity.
